entries.what_it_does: Ctrl+Z reverses the last action. Ctrl+Y reapplies it. Together they form the primary safety net for all Excel operations, enabling fearless experimentation without risk of permanent data loss.
entries.intent: When you make a mistake or want to reverse a recent change, use Ctrl+Z to undo and Ctrl+Y to redo without touching the mouse.
entries.example:
Ctrl+Z     -- Undo last action (up to 100 steps)
Ctrl+Y     -- Redo (reapply undone action)
Ctrl+Z x3  -- Undo last 3 actions in sequence

Note: Saving the file (Ctrl+S) does NOT clear the undo stack.
Note: Some VBA macros clear the undo stack on execution.
entries.failure_modes: Undo stack is cleared by: saving with certain file formats, running VBA macros, some Power Query refreshes. Undo does not reverse sheet protection changes or name manager edits.
entries.performance_notes: Excel maintains up to 100 undo steps by default. Reducing this in advanced settings speeds up large workbooks at the cost of undo history depth.
entries.governance_notes: The foundation of safe Excel work. Teach Ctrl+Z before any function. The undo stack is your first audit trail — it records every change in the session.

entries.name: Ctrl+Arrow (Range Navigation)
entries.what_it_does: Jumps to the last non-empty cell in the direction of the arrow key, enabling instant navigation to the end of a data range without scrolling or mouse use.
entries.intent: When you need to navigate quickly to the edge of a data range or find the last row of a dataset, use Ctrl+Arrow to jump instantly without scrolling.
entries.example:
Ctrl+Down  -- Jump to last filled cell in column
Ctrl+Right -- Jump to last filled cell in row
Ctrl+End   -- Jump to last used cell in worksheet
Ctrl+Home  -- Jump to cell A1

Shift+Ctrl+Down -- Select from current cell to last filled cell
(Essential for selecting dynamic-length ranges)
entries.failure_modes: Blank rows interrupt the jump — Ctrl+Down stops at the blank, not the true end. In tables (Ctrl+T), Ctrl+Down correctly navigates to the last row of the table regardless of blanks.
entries.performance_notes: Zero computational impact. Pure navigation. The speed gain is entirely in human workflow — eliminates scrolling on large datasets.
entries.governance_notes: Shift+Ctrl+Down for dynamic range selection replaces hardcoded range references in many scenarios. Use it before writing any formula that references 'the whole column'.

entries.name: ISNUMBER / ISTEXT / ISBLANK
entries.what_it_does: Type-checking functions that return TRUE/FALSE based on whether a cell contains a number, text, or is empty — enabling conditional logic that responds to data type rather than value.
entries.intent: When formula logic must behave differently based on what type of data a cell contains, use ISNUMBER, ISTEXT, or ISBLANK to test the data type before processing.
entries.example:
=IF(ISNUMBER(A2), A2*Rate, 0)          -- Skip non-numeric
=IF(ISBLANK(B2), 'Pending', B2)        -- Default for blanks
=SUMPRODUCT(ISNUMBER(Range)*1)         -- Count numeric cells
=IF(ISTEXT(A2), UPPER(A2), TEXT(A2,'0')) -- Handle either type
entries.failure_modes: ISNUMBER returns FALSE for numbers stored as text — one of the most common silent data quality failures. Use ISNUMBER(VALUE(A2)) to catch text-formatted numbers.
entries.performance_notes: All IS-functions evaluate instantly. SUMPRODUCT(ISNUMBER(range)*1) is the fastest way to count numeric cells without a helper column.
entries.governance_notes: Use type-checking functions to make formulas defensive against mixed data types in input columns. A formula that crashes on text input is a formula waiting to fail in production.

entries.name: EDATE / EOMONTH
entries.what_it_does: EDATE returns a date a specified number of months before or after a start date. EOMONTH returns the last day of the month offset by a given number of months — both enabling governed date arithmetic.
entries.intent: When you need to calculate future or past dates by month rather than by day, use EDATE for same-day offsets and EOMONTH for month-end calculations.
entries.example:
=EDATE(A2, 3)           -- 3 months after date in A2
=EDATE(A2, -12)         -- 1 year before date in A2
=EOMONTH(A2, 0)         -- Last day of same month
=EOMONTH(A2, 1)         -- Last day of next month
=EOMONTH(A2, 0)+1       -- First day of next month
entries.failure_modes: EDATE returns a serial number — format the result as a date or wrap in TEXT(). Both functions require the Analysis ToolPak in Excel 2007 and earlier.
entries.performance_notes: Both functions are fast and non-volatile. Preferred over manual day-arithmetic (+30, +31) which produces wrong results across month boundaries.
entries.governance_notes: Use EOMONTH for all period-end date calculations in DDL workbooks. Never calculate month-end dates with + 30 or + 31 — month lengths vary. EDATE is the governed alternative to date + 30.

entries.name: KPI Card Pattern
entries.what_it_does: Structures dashboard summary metrics as individual governed cells combining a primary value, a comparison delta (MoM or YoY), a directional indicator, and a label — creating scannable executive-level summary cards.
entries.intent: When a dashboard needs to surface key metrics at a glance, use the KPI card pattern to display each metric with its context (trend, delta, label) in a compact, consistent cell group.
entries.example:
// KPI card components (4-cell group):
Label:    'Revenue'                    -- Static label
Value:    =SUMIFS(...)                 -- Current period value
Delta:    =(Current/Prior)-1           -- Period-over-period change
Trend:    =IF(Delta>0,'▲','▼')         -- Directional indicator

// CF rule on delta cell:
=C4>0 → green fill  |  =C4<0 → red fill
entries.failure_modes: Hardcoding the comparison period in the delta formula — use a named sel_ parameter so the period can change without formula edits. KPI cards that don't show comparison context are data without meaning.
entries.performance_notes: KPI cards are simple aggregation formulas. No performance concerns at normal scale.
entries.governance_notes: Every DDL dashboard must have at minimum three KPI cards per STD-EXCEL-001 Rule 6. Each card must show: value, period label, and directional delta. A number without context is not a KPI.

entries.name: Lookup Table Pattern
entries.what_it_does: Centralizes reference data (rates, categories, mappings, thresholds) in dedicated, named Excel Tables on a Ref_ sheet, enabling all formulas to look up values by key rather than hardcoding them or duplicating them across sheets.
entries.intent: When multiple formulas reference the same set of values (rates, tiers, categories), use the lookup table pattern to centralize that data in a governed Ref_ table so changes propagate everywhere automatically.
entries.example:
// Ref_CommissionRates table:
Tier     | Rate
Bronze   | 0.05
Silver   | 0.08
Gold     | 0.12

// Formula using it:
=XLOOKUP(SalesRep[Tier],
  Ref_CommissionRates[Tier],
  Ref_CommissionRates[Rate], 0)
entries.failure_modes: Lookup tables without a Ref_ prefix get confused with fact tables. Missing rows in the lookup table cause silent #N/A failures — always include an if_not_found argument in XLOOKUP.
entries.performance_notes: Small lookup tables (under 1K rows) have no performance cost. For large lookup tables, sort by key and use binary search mode in XLOOKUP.
entries.governance_notes: Every set of values used in more than one formula must live in a lookup table. This is the intersection of the star schema principle (PTN-0001) and the anti-hardcoding rule (ANT-0003). Named ranges on lookup columns enable self-documenting formulas.

entries.name: Tab Color Coding
entries.what_it_does: Assigns consistent colors to worksheet tabs based on their layer role (INPUT=blue, LOGIC=gray, OUTPUT=green, CONTROL=yellow, STAGING=orange, DASHBOARD=purple) making workbook structure immediately visible from the tab bar.
entries.intent: When a workbook has multiple tabs serving different architectural roles, use tab color coding to make the layer structure visible without opening each sheet.
entries.example:
Right-click tab → Tab Color
INPUT:     Light Blue   (#BDD7EE)
LOGIC:     Light Gray   (#D9D9D9)
OUTPUT:    Light Green  (#E2EFDA)
CONTROL:   Light Yellow (#FFEB9C)
STAGING:   Light Orange (#FCE4D6)
DASHBOARD: Light Purple (#E2C9FF)
README:    No color (white — first tab)
entries.failure_modes: Inconsistent color assignments across workbooks defeat the purpose. Establish the convention once (in a README or standards doc) and apply it everywhere.
entries.performance_notes: Zero computational impact. Tab colors are display metadata only.
entries.governance_notes: Required in all DDL operational workbooks per STD-EXCEL-002 Rule 8. A person who has never seen the workbook should understand its architecture from the tab bar alone. The color is the documentation.

entries.name: Column Header Convention
entries.what_it_does: Establishes consistent rules for column header formatting: PascalCase or snake_case naming, no spaces, no special characters, type suffix for ambiguous columns (e.g., _Date, _ID, _Amt), and a single frozen header row at row 1 of every table.
entries.intent: When naming columns in any Excel Table or data range, use the column header convention to ensure names are Power Query-compatible, formula-referenceable, and self-describing.
entries.example:
CORRECT:
  SalesRep_ID    OrderDate      Revenue_Amt
  Product_Name   Status         Commission_Rate

WRONG:
  Sales Rep ID   (spaces -- breaks structured refs)
  date           (ambiguous -- which date?)
  col1           (meaningless)
  Revenue ($)    (special chars -- breaks PQ)
entries.failure_modes: Column headers with spaces require bracket escaping in structured references: =Table[[Sales Rep]]  — error-prone and unreadable. Special characters break Power Query column references silently.
entries.performance_notes: No computational impact. The value is entirely in formula readability, Power Query compatibility, and reduced debugging time.
entries.governance_notes: All DDL table columns must use PascalCase or snake_case with no spaces. Type suffixes (_ID, _Date, _Amt, _Flag, _Txt) are required for columns whose type is not obvious from the name alone.

entries.name: Rainbow Formatting
entries.what_it_does: Applies inconsistent, arbitrary cell colors, font colors, and highlight patterns as personal annotation rather than governed visual signals — creating visual noise that obscures structure and confuses other users.
entries.intent: When you need to visually annotate a workbook, use governed conditional formatting rules and tab color conventions instead of manual rainbow formatting that carries no transferable meaning.
entries.example:
WRONG:
Yellow = 'I need to check this'
Green  = 'Looks good'
Red    = 'Problem -- maybe'
Blue   = 'Sent to someone'
(Meaning exists only in the builder's head)

RIGHT:
Conditional formatting rule: =Status='Exception' → red
All colors derive from data, not manual annotation
entries.failure_modes: Manual colors are invisible to formulas, Power Query, and any automated process. A filter cannot find 'yellow cells' without VBA. Manual colors also survive copy-paste, spreading meaningless formatting throughout downstream workbooks.
entries.performance_notes: Manual cell formatting has negligible performance cost, but the human cost of decoding undocumented color conventions is unbounded.
entries.governance_notes: Manual cell coloring is banned in DDL workbooks except for the three governed uses: input cell indicators (blue fill), tab color coding (CON-0008), and temporary build scaffolding (removed before sharing). All permanent visual signals must come from conditional formatting rules.

entries.name: Workbook Versioning Pattern
entries.what_it_does: Establishes a governed file naming and internal versioning system for Excel workbooks: filename includes version suffix (_v1, _v2), a VERSION cell on the README tab tracks the current version, and a change log records every structural modification.
entries.intent: When a workbook will go through multiple iterations or be shared with stakeholders, use the workbook versioning pattern to make the current state unambiguous and all changes traceable.
entries.example:
Filename: Excelligence_Registry_v012.xlsx

README tab VERSION cell:
  Version:     v0.1.2
  Released:    2026-03-17
  Status:      Active

Change Log table columns:
  Version | Date | Author | Change | Reason
entries.failure_modes: Version numbers in filename only (not inside the workbook) — the file can be renamed without updating internal state. Change logs without mandatory entries become optional and therefore useless.
entries.performance_notes: No performance impact. The versioning infrastructure is metadata — it has zero effect on calculation speed.
entries.governance_notes: Every DDL workbook that is shared externally or has more than one version must use this pattern. The README tab VERSION cell is the single source of truth for what version is open. Filename can lie. The cell cannot.

entries.name: Numbers Stored as Text
entries.what_it_does: Cells containing numeric values stored as text strings cause SUM, AVERAGE, and VLOOKUP to silently ignore or misread those cells without producing a visible error.
entries.intent: When you inherit a workbook with green triangle warnings or formulas returning zero on populated ranges, use VALUE() or Power Query type enforcement to convert text-stored numbers to proper numeric type.
entries.example:
WRONG: Column D shows 1000, 2000, 3000 but =SUM(D:D) returns 0. Cells are left-aligned — they are text.

RIGHT:
=VALUE(D2) in a helper column, then paste-special values over the source.
OR in Power Query: Transform > Data Type > Whole Number.
entries.failure_modes: Silent zero in SUM/AVERAGE; #N/A on VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP; incorrect sort order (text sort vs numeric sort); conditional formatting thresholds never trigger.
entries.performance_notes: No performance overhead once corrected. The problem is invisible until totals are audited.
entries.governance_notes: Numbers Stored as Text is the single most common invisible failure in inherited workbooks. The green triangle indicator is Excel's only signal — and it is routinely ignored. Any PQ import must explicitly cast numeric columns at the type enforcement step.

entries.name: Hidden Rows/Columns as Logic
entries.what_it_does: Business logic, intermediate calculations, or reference variables are buried in hidden rows or columns instead of being modeled explicitly, creating invisible dependencies that break when anyone unhides and edits them.
entries.intent: When intermediate calculations are required, use a dedicated calculation sheet or named ranges so the logic is visible, auditable, and protected rather than concealed inside the presentation layer.
entries.example:
WRONG: Row 47 is hidden. It contains the commission rate used in rows 10-46. A colleague unhides and deletes it. All commission calculations return #REF!.

RIGHT: Commission rate lives in a named cell (CommRate) on the Config tab. Formula: =Revenue * CommRate.
entries.failure_modes: #REF! cascade when hidden rows/columns are deleted; silent wrong result when hidden cells are inadvertently overwritten; workbook appears self-contained but is not.
entries.performance_notes: Zero overhead. This is a structural and auditability issue, not a performance issue.
entries.governance_notes: Hidden rows and columns are presentation tools, not logic containers. Any calculation that drives visible output must live in a named range, a config tab, or a governed calculation layer — never hidden inside the presentation sheet.

entries.name: Color as Data
entries.what_it_does: Fill color, font color, or conditional formatting is used as the primary data signal (e.g., green = approved, red = rejected) instead of an explicit status field, making the data unsortable, unfiltered, and invisible to formulas.
entries.intent: When you need to communicate status or category, use an explicit text or numeric field with controlled vocabulary so the status can drive formulas, filters, pivot tables, and exports without VBA.
entries.example:
WRONG: Rows highlighted in red are flagged for review. No column encodes this. You cannot filter, count, or export the red rows without a macro.

RIGHT: Add a Status column with values [Approved / Flagged / Pending]. Apply conditional formatting as a view layer over the Status column value.
entries.failure_modes: Status is invisible to COUNTIF/SUMIF/FILTER; copy-paste strips formatting and loses the data signal; exports to CSV or Power BI lose all color information; no audit trail of status changes.
entries.performance_notes: Explicit status fields are faster to calculate against than color-based formulas (GET.CELL workarounds are volatile).
entries.governance_notes: Color is a view layer, not a data layer. Every status, category, or flag that drives business logic must exist as an explicit field. Conditional formatting is permitted as a secondary visual layer over a real field value — never as the primary signal.

entries.name: Broken External Links
entries.what_it_does: Workbook formulas reference external files that are missing, renamed, moved, or path-bound to one machine, causing #REF! errors or stale values that update only when the source file is open.
entries.intent: When data must come from an external source, use Power Query's controlled import layer to load and stage the data locally, eliminating live external link dependencies from formula cells.
entries.example:
WRONG: ='C:\\Users\\john\\Desktop\\[Budget.xlsx]Sheet1'!$B$4 — works on John's machine only.

RIGHT: Power Query connection to Budget.xlsx staged to a local table. Formula references the local table. Refresh is deliberate and logged.
entries.failure_modes: File opens with 'Update links?' prompt that users dismiss, leaving stale values indefinitely; #REF! when source file is moved or renamed; path breaks when workbook is shared.
entries.performance_notes: External links recalculate every time the workbook opens if source files are available, adding open time on large link networks.
entries.governance_notes: External links in formula cells are a governance failure. Any data sourced externally must flow through a controlled import layer (Power Query or a manual paste-and-stamp pattern with a date stamp cell). Formula cells must reference local tables only.

entries.name: Copy-Paste Values Over Formulas
entries.what_it_does: Live formula cells are overwritten with paste-special values to freeze a result, destroying the calculation chain and making the workbook a dead artifact that cannot be updated, audited, or reproduced.
entries.intent: When a calculated result must be preserved at a point in time, use a dedicated archive tab with a timestamp and paste-values pattern documented in a README, keeping the source calculation sheet intact.
entries.example:
WRONG: Month-end close. Controller pastes values over all formula cells to 'lock' the numbers. Next month, no one can trace how the numbers were derived.

RIGHT: Archive tab pattern — formulas stay in the Calc tab, values-only snapshot copies to an Archive_YYYYMM tab with a timestamp and the operator's name.
entries.failure_modes: Calculation chain destroyed; no way to reproduce historical results; future maintainers cannot understand derivation; a single paste-special wipes months of formula work.
entries.performance_notes: Paste-values is irreversible without version control. Git or SharePoint version history is the only recovery path.
entries.governance_notes: Paste-values is a valid technique in one context only: intentional archiving with documentation. In all other contexts it is a destructive operation. Any workbook under governance must have a README that defines the archive pattern and prohibits ad-hoc paste-values on formula tabs.

entries.name: Over-Nesting
entries.what_it_does: Multiple levels of nested functions — especially nested IFs — create formulas that are unreadable, impossible to debug, and break in non-obvious ways when conditions change.
entries.intent: When logic requires multiple branching conditions, use LET to name intermediate results, IFS for flat multi-condition evaluation, or helper columns to decompose the logic into auditable steps.
entries.example:
WRONG: =IF(A2>100,IF(B2="Y",IF(C2<>"Z","Tier1","Tier2"),"Tier3"),IF(A2>50,"Tier4","Tier5"))

RIGHT (IFS): =IFS(AND(A2>100,B2="Y",C2<>"Z"),"Tier1",AND(A2>100,B2="Y"),"Tier2",...)
OR decompose into Status, Band, and Category helper columns — one condition per column.
entries.failure_modes: Debug time scales exponentially with nesting depth; wrong branch fires silently; Excel 2019 and earlier have a 64-nest limit; LET is unavailable in older versions requiring a rewrite.
entries.performance_notes: Nested IFs evaluate every branch until a match. IFS and LET are faster on large ranges. Helper columns are fastest of all — each cell calculates once.
entries.governance_notes: Maximum nesting depth in DDL workbooks: 2 levels. Any logic requiring more than 2 levels of nesting must be decomposed into helper columns or a lookup table. Nested IFs beyond 2 deep are a code smell indicating a missing data structure.

entries.name: Sheet-as-Database
entries.what_it_does: A worksheet is used as a flat-file database — rows appended indefinitely without table structure, headers that drift, no declared grain, and mixed data types per column — until the workbook becomes a data swamp that no formula can reliably query.
entries.intent: When collecting transactional or operational data, convert the range to an Excel Table (Ctrl+T) with a declared grain, typed columns, and a consistent header row so the data is queryable, filterable, and safe to reference from formulas.
entries.example:
WRONG: Sheet 'Data' has 4,000 rows. Row 1 says 'Name / Dept / Amount / Date'. Row 847 has a sub-header 'Q3 Entries'. Column D has dates in rows 1-400, then text notes in rows 401+.

RIGHT: One grain per table ('one row = one transaction'). Ctrl+T converts to Table. Column types enforced. Sub-headers removed — filtering handles grouping.
entries.failure_modes: VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP returns wrong results when headers drift; SUMIF misses rows with sub-headers; Power Query load produces type errors on mixed columns; new rows below the table are not included in formulas.
entries.performance_notes: Excel Tables auto-expand formulas to new rows. Unstructured ranges do not. The maintenance cost of a data swamp grows with every row.
entries.governance_notes: Every data collection tab in a DDL workbook must be an Excel Table with a declared grain. 'One row = one [entity]' must be documented in the README. Mixed-type columns and drifting headers are blocking issues in any workbook review.

entries.name: Blanket Error Masking
entries.what_it_does: Wrapping large or untested formula blocks in IFERROR(..., "") hides structural failures — #N/A from broken lookups, #REF! from deleted ranges, #DIV/0! from missing denominators — under silent blank cells that appear correct.
entries.intent: When a formula has a known, expected failure mode, use IFERROR with a specific fallback value and document the condition; for XLOOKUP use the if_not_found argument directly rather than wrapping the entire expression.
entries.example:
WRONG: =IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2,Data!B:F,4,0),"") — masks both 'not found' (expected) and 'wrong column index' (structural bug).

RIGHT: =XLOOKUP(A2,Data[ID],Data[Amount],"Not Found",0) — handles expected miss explicitly. #REF! from a structural error is still visible.
entries.failure_modes: Structural errors (wrong column, deleted range, circular reference) look identical to valid lookups; dashboard shows zeros or blanks where values should exist; bugs survive QA because the error is invisible.
entries.performance_notes: IFERROR evaluates the formula twice on error — once to get the error, once to return the fallback. XLOOKUP's native if_not_found argument is single-evaluation and faster.
entries.governance_notes: IFERROR is a governed tool for expected exceptions only. It must never wrap a formula that has not been tested for structural correctness. Every IFERROR in a DDL workbook requires a comment stating which specific error condition it is designed to handle.

entries.name: LET
entries.what_it_does: Assigns names to intermediate calculation results within a single formula, eliminating repeated sub-expressions, improving readability, and making complex formulas debuggable without helper columns.
entries.intent: When a formula evaluates the same sub-expression more than once or contains logic that cannot be understood in a single read, use LET to name each component so the formula reads like documented code.
entries.example:
WITHOUT LET (repeated calculation):
=IF(XLOOKUP(A2,T[ID],T[Amt],0)*1.1>1000, XLOOKUP(A2,T[ID],T[Amt],0)*1.1, 0)

WITH LET:
=LET(
  raw, XLOOKUP(A2,T[ID],T[Amt],0),
  adjusted, raw*1.1,
  IF(adjusted>1000, adjusted, 0)
)
entries.failure_modes: LET requires Excel 365 or Excel 2021 — not available in Excel 2019 or earlier; variable names cannot conflict with cell addresses or Excel function names.
entries.performance_notes: Each named variable is evaluated once regardless of how many times it appears in the body, making LET faster than repeated sub-expressions on large ranges.
entries.governance_notes: LET is the preferred pattern for any formula longer than 80 characters. Named variables must describe what they represent, not how they are calculated (e.g., 'adjusted_revenue' not 'B2_times_1point1').

entries.name: Unpivot Columns
entries.what_it_does: Transforms wide-format data where months, categories, or entities are column headers into tall-format data where each value has its own row, which is the required structure for PivotTables, SUMIFS, and dimensional analysis.
entries.intent: When you receive a report-style table with time periods or categories as column headers, use Power Query Unpivot to normalize it into a fact-table structure before loading to Excel.
entries.example:
BEFORE (wide):
Region | Jan | Feb | Mar
North  | 100 | 120 | 90
South  | 80  | 95  | 110

AFTER (tall, unpivoted):
Region | Month | Amount
North  | Jan   | 100
North  | Feb   | 120
...

In PQ: Select Jan/Feb/Mar columns > Transform > Unpivot Columns.
entries.failure_modes: Unpivoting columns that should remain as attributes (e.g., Name, ID) — select only the value columns before unpivoting; unpivoting creates an 'Attribute' column that needs renaming.
entries.performance_notes: Unpivot runs at query load time. The resulting tall table is significantly more efficient for PivotTables and SUMIFS than wide formats.
entries.governance_notes: Any source data in wide/cross-tab format must be unpivoted in the Power Query transformation layer before loading to the model. Wide-format data must never be the source for SUMIFS or PivotTable analysis — it will produce incorrect totals.

entries.name: Input-Calc-Output Separation
entries.what_it_does: Organizes a workbook into three distinct zones — an Input sheet (user-editable, validated), a Calculation sheet (formula-only, no manual entry), and an Output sheet (dashboards and exports) — preventing the single-sheet-does-everything anti-pattern.
entries.intent: When building any workbook that will be maintained or shared, apply Input-Calc-Output separation at the tab level so inputs, logic, and presentation are physically separated and independently protected.
entries.example:
Tab structure:
  INPUT_  — Blue tabs. Validated data entry cells only.
  CALC_   — Gray tabs. Formulas only. Sheet protected.
  DASH_   — Green tabs. Linked to CALC_ output ranges. No formulas.
  CONFIG_ — Amber tab. Named constants and parameters.

Rule: No manual values in CALC_ tabs. No formulas in DASH_ tabs.
entries.failure_modes: Mixing input and calculation on the same sheet; using dashboard tabs as formula intermediaries; failing to protect CALC_ sheets allowing accidental overwrites.
entries.performance_notes: Separated architecture makes recalculation trees predictable. Excel calculates in dependency order — clean separation means the calc chain is clear.
entries.governance_notes: Input-Calc-Output is the foundational architectural pattern for all DDL workbooks. Tab color convention (CON-0010) enforces it visually. Sheet protection enforces it structurally. Any workbook that mixes input and calculation on the same sheet requires architectural remediation before it can be considered governed.

entries.name: Helper Column Decomposition
entries.what_it_does: Breaks a single complex or opaque formula into a sequence of named intermediate columns, each performing one step, so the logic is visible, testable, and auditable without requiring LET or VBA.
entries.intent: When a formula exceeds one logical step or cannot be understood in a single read, decompose it into helper columns with descriptive headers so each step can be independently verified.
entries.example:
INSTEAD OF: =IF(AND(DATEDIF(B2,TODAY(),"Y")>=2,C2="Active",VLOOKUP(D2,Rates!A:B,2,0)>500),"Eligible","Not Eligible")

DECOMPOSED:
Col E (Years_Tenure): =DATEDIF(B2,TODAY(),"Y")
Col F (Is_Active): =C2="Active"
Col G (Rate): =VLOOKUP(D2,Rates!A:B,2,0)
Col H (Eligible): =IF(AND(E2>=2,F2,G2>500),"Eligible","Not Eligible")
entries.failure_modes: Helper columns must be protected or clearly labeled to prevent editing; decomposed columns that are deleted break the final formula; over-decomposition creates too many columns — judge by whether each step adds audit value.
entries.performance_notes: Helper columns calculate sequentially and are cached. They are faster than repeated sub-expressions in a single mega-formula and significantly easier to profile for errors.
entries.governance_notes: Helper Column Decomposition is the zero-dependency alternative to LET — it works in all Excel versions and is the preferred pattern when the workbook will be maintained by users without Excel 365. Any formula requiring over-nesting must be decomposed before it can be considered governed.

entries.name: Structured References
entries.what_it_does: Replaces raw cell addresses (B2:B100) with table-based column references (Table1[Amount]) that automatically expand when the table grows, remain readable without a cell map, and survive column insertion without breaking.
entries.intent: When referencing data inside an Excel Table, use structured references (Table[Column]) instead of range addresses so formulas are self-documenting and resilient to structural changes.
entries.example:
FRAGILE: =SUMIF(B:B,"North",D:D)
GOVERNED: =SUMIF(Sales[Region],"North",Sales[Amount])

When a column is inserted between B and D, the fragile version silently returns wrong results. The structured version updates automatically.
entries.failure_modes: Structured references only work inside or referencing Excel Tables — ranges must be Ctrl+T converted first; [@Column] inside a table refers to the same-row cell; Table[Column] outside refers to the full column.
entries.performance_notes: Excel optimizes structured references as part of table column metadata. They are marginally faster than range-based references on large tables.
entries.governance_notes: All formula references to tabular data in DDL workbooks must use structured references. Raw A1 or R1C1 addresses are permitted only for scalar references (single named cells on a Config tab). Any formula containing B2:B1000 in a governed workbook is a remediation candidate.

entries.name: Driver Table Architecture
entries.what_it_does: Centralizes all configurable business logic — rate tables, threshold mappings, category toggles, scenario flags — in one governed reference table so changes require editing one cell rather than hunting through formulas.
entries.intent: When a workbook contains business rules that change over time (rates, thresholds, category assignments), centralize them in a Driver Table so formulas look up the rule rather than hardcoding it.
entries.example:
DRIVER TABLE (Config tab):
Parameter       | Value
Tax_Rate        | 0.0875
Tier1_Threshold | 500
Tier2_Threshold | 1000
Approval_Limit  | 5000

FORMULA: =Revenue * XLOOKUP("Tax_Rate", Config[Parameter], Config[Value])

To change the tax rate: edit one cell. Every formula updates automatically.
entries.failure_modes: Driver table must be on a protected sheet with only the Value column unlocked; parameter names must be stable — renaming a parameter breaks all formulas referencing it; XLOOKUP on driver table returns #N/A if parameter name has trailing spaces.
entries.performance_notes: Driver Table adds one XLOOKUP call per formula reference. Negligible on < 10,000 rows. For high-volume calculations, use named ranges pointing to the driver table cells instead of XLOOKUP.
entries.governance_notes: Any value that appears more than once in a workbook belongs in the Driver Table. Any value that might change between reporting periods belongs in the Driver Table. Hardcoded values in formula cells that match a known business parameter are a governance finding.

entries.name: Tab Color Convention
entries.what_it_does: Assigns consistent colors to worksheet tabs by functional zone — inputs, calculations, outputs, config, archive — so workbook architecture is immediately visible at the tab bar without opening any sheet.
entries.intent: When building any workbook that will be maintained or shared, apply tab colors by functional zone so collaborators can navigate by color and so the Input-Calc-Output separation is visually enforced.
entries.example:
DDL Tab Color Standard:
  Blue   (#4472C4) — INPUT_ tabs (user entry)
  Gray   (#808080) — CALC_ tabs (formulas only)
  Green  (#70AD47) — DASH_ tabs (dashboards/output)
  Amber  (#ED7D31) — CONFIG_ tab (parameters/driver table)
  Red    (#FF0000) — ARCHIVE_ tabs (frozen snapshots)
  White  (none)   — README or Documentation tabs

Apply: Right-click tab > Tab Color
entries.failure_modes: Color alone is not sufficient governance — sheet protection and naming conventions are required; Excel prints do not show tab colors; tab colors are not preserved in CSV export.
entries.performance_notes: Zero computational impact. Pure navigational convention.
entries.governance_notes: Tab Color Convention is the visual enforcement layer for Input-Calc-Output Separation (ARC-0009). It must be applied at workbook creation, not retrofitted. Any governed DDL workbook without tab colors applied is incomplete.

entries.name: IFERROR
entries.what_it_does: Returns a specified value when a formula produces any error (#N/A, #VALUE!, #REF!, #DIV/0!, etc.), preventing error cascades into dependent cells while allowing the builder to define graceful fallback behavior.
entries.intent: When a formula has a known, expected failure condition, wrap it with IFERROR and a specific fallback value — but only after the formula has been tested for structural correctness.
entries.example:
EXPECTED MISS (correct use):
=IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2, RefTable, 2, 0), "Not in reference table")

STRUCTURAL MASK (incorrect use — see ANT-0016):
=IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2, B:F, 4, 0), "") — masks both expected misses AND wrong column index bugs.

MODERN ALTERNATIVE:
=XLOOKUP(A2, RefTable[ID], RefTable[Name], "Not in reference table") — no wrapper needed.
entries.failure_modes: IFERROR masks all error types indiscriminately — structural bugs (wrong column, deleted sheet) look identical to expected misses; empty string fallback is indistinguishable from a real empty cell in downstream analysis.
entries.performance_notes: IFERROR evaluates the formula twice on error (once to produce the error, once to return the fallback). XLOOKUP's native if_not_found is single-evaluation. Prefer XLOOKUP over IFERROR-wrapped VLOOKUP.
entries.governance_notes: IFERROR is a governed tool for expected exceptions only. It must never wrap a formula that has not been tested. Every IFERROR in a DDL workbook requires a comment or helper column note stating which specific error condition it handles. See ANT-0016 for the anti-pattern version.

entries.name: UNIQUE
entries.what_it_does: Returns a dynamic spill array of distinct values from a range, automatically updating when source data changes, replacing manual Remove Duplicates operations and static unique-value lists.
entries.intent: When you need a deduplicated list of values for a dropdown, a summary table, or a distinct-count analysis, use UNIQUE to generate a live, auto-updating array instead of a static copy.
entries.example:
=UNIQUE(Sales[Region])
Spills a dynamic list of unique regions from the Sales table.
Update the source data — the unique list updates automatically.

COMBINED:
=SORT(UNIQUE(Sales[Region])) — sorted unique list
=COUNTA(UNIQUE(Sales[Region])) — count of distinct regions
entries.failure_modes: #SPILL! if the spill range is blocked by existing content; UNIQUE requires Excel 365 or Excel 2021 — not available in Excel 2019 or earlier; returns all unique rows when given multiple columns.
entries.performance_notes: UNIQUE is a dynamic array function — it spills automatically and recalculates when source data changes. More efficient than COUNTIF-based dedup patterns on large ranges.
entries.governance_notes: UNIQUE replaces all manual Remove Duplicates operations in governed workbooks. Any static unique-value list that is manually maintained must be replaced with UNIQUE + SORT on the source column. Pairs with FILTER for conditional distinct-value extraction.

entries.name: Ctrl+Shift+L — Toggle AutoFilter
entries.what_it_does: Instantly enables or disables AutoFilter on the active range or table, the fastest path to sorting and filtering any dataset without touching the ribbon.
entries.intent: When you need to filter or sort any data range, use Ctrl+Shift+L to toggle AutoFilter rather than navigating to the Data ribbon, saving navigation time on every analysis session.
entries.example:
1. Click any cell inside a data range
2. Ctrl+Shift+L — filter dropdowns appear on headers
3. Click any filter dropdown to sort or filter
4. Ctrl+Shift+L again — filters removed, dropdowns gone

Works on ranges and Excel Tables. On a Table (Ctrl+T), filter arrows are always visible — Ctrl+Shift+L shows/hides them.
entries.failure_modes: Does not work if the active cell is outside any contiguous data range; removing AutoFilter also clears active filter criteria — all rows become visible.
entries.performance_notes: Keyboard shortcut. Zero computational overhead beyond the filter operation itself.
entries.governance_notes: Ctrl+Shift+L is the entry-point shortcut for data analysis. Pair with Ctrl+T (table conversion) — Tables have persistent filter arrows that respond to Ctrl+Shift+L without needing to re-enable AutoFilter each session.

entries.name: GROUPBY
entries.what_it_does: Aggregates a dataset by grouping rows on one or more columns and applying a function (SUM, AVERAGE, COUNT, etc.) to return a dynamic summary table without a PivotTable.
entries.intent: When you need a dynamic summary table that groups data by one or more dimensions and updates automatically, use GROUPBY to replace static PivotTables in formula-driven architectures.
entries.example:
=GROUPBY(Sales[Region], Sales[Amount], SUM)
Returns a two-column spill: Region | Total Amount
Updates automatically when source data changes.

=GROUPBY(Sales[Region], Sales[Amount], SUM, , , 1)
Sorted descending by total amount.
entries.failure_modes: Available in Excel 365 only — not in Excel 2021 or earlier; requires a flat table structure as input, not a PivotTable; empty rows in source cause grouping errors; result cannot be directly referenced by cell address — must use spill range operator (#).
entries.performance_notes: GROUPBY is calculated engine-side and is significantly faster than equivalent SUMIFS arrays on large datasets. More efficient than a PivotTable for formula-driven dashboards because it recalculates in dependency order without requiring manual refresh.
entries.governance_notes: GROUPBY is the governed replacement for PivotTable-based summaries in formula-driven workbooks. Use GROUPBY when the summary must update automatically within a formula chain. Use PivotTables when the user needs interactive slicing. Never combine GROUPBY output with raw SUMIFS on the same dataset — one source of truth per summary.

entries.name: PIVOTBY
entries.what_it_does: Creates a pivot-style cross-tabulation with row and column grouping in a single dynamic array formula, producing a two-dimensional summary table that updates automatically.
entries.intent: When you need a cross-tabulated summary (rows AND columns both grouped) that updates automatically without a PivotTable, use PIVOTBY as the formula-native pivot engine.
entries.example:
=PIVOTBY(Sales[Region], Sales[Quarter], Sales[Amount], SUM)
Returns a grid: Regions as rows, Quarters as columns, SUM of Amount at each intersection.
Updates when source data changes — no Refresh needed.
entries.failure_modes: Excel 365 only — not available in earlier versions; both row and column fields must be single columns; result spills into a variable range — ensure sufficient blank space below and to the right; cannot be nested inside most other functions.
entries.performance_notes: PIVOTBY is the most efficient way to produce a cross-tabulation in a formula chain. Significantly faster than equivalent SUMIFS matrix arrays. Comparable to PivotTable performance on datasets under 500K rows.
entries.governance_notes: PIVOTBY is the governed alternative to manually built SUMIFS cross-tabulation matrices. Any workbook using a grid of SUMIFS formulas to produce a cross-tab should be migrated to PIVOTBY. Pairs with GROUPBY — use GROUPBY for one-dimensional summaries, PIVOTBY for two-dimensional. Both require Excel 365.

entries.name: MAKEARRAY
entries.what_it_does: Builds a two-dimensional array of specified dimensions by applying a LAMBDA function to each row and column index, enabling custom matrix construction without helper columns or VBA.
entries.intent: When you need to generate a custom two-dimensional array where each cell's value depends on its row and column position, use MAKEARRAY with a LAMBDA to define the per-cell calculation.
entries.example:
-- Multiplication table
=MAKEARRAY(10, 10, LAMBDA(r, c, r*c))
Generates a 10x10 multiplication table dynamically.

-- Conditional count matrix (from the Assurance Map)
=MAKEARRAY(
  ROWS(rowsDim), COLUMNS(qtrs),
  LAMBDA(r, c, COUNTIFS(dimKey, INDEX(rowsDim,r), QuarterTag, INDEX(qtrs,c)))
)
entries.failure_modes: Excel 365 only; LAMBDA inside MAKEARRAY cannot reference cells directly — must use INDEX to extract values from arrays; result spills into a variable range — no other content can occupy that area; nesting additional LAMBDAs inside the MAKEARRAY LAMBDA increases complexity rapidly.
entries.performance_notes: MAKEARRAY is evaluated as a single array operation — more efficient than equivalent nested BYROW/BYCOL combinations for fixed-dimension outputs. On large dimensions (100x100+) recalculation can be slow — consider Power Query for static large matrices.
entries.governance_notes: MAKEARRAY is an Expert-tier function reserved for cases where no simpler pattern produces the required two-dimensional output. Before using MAKEARRAY, confirm that GROUPBY, PIVOTBY, or a PivotTable cannot solve the problem. Every MAKEARRAY formula must be documented with a comment or README entry explaining the row/column semantics. See ANT-0014 (Over-Nesting) for the risk of nesting MAKEARRAY inside other complex functions.

entries.name: EDATE
entries.what_it_does: Returns the date that is a specified number of months before or after a given start date, enabling dynamic date range construction without hardcoded month offsets.
entries.intent: When you need to calculate a date a fixed number of months from a reference date — for rolling windows, maturity dates, or period end calculations — use EDATE to keep the calculation dynamic and version-independent.
entries.example:
-- 3 months after contract start
=EDATE(A2, 3)

-- Last day of current month
=EDATE(DATE(YEAR(TODAY()), MONTH(TODAY())+1, 1), 0)-1

-- Rolling 12-month window start
=EDATE(TODAY(), -12)
Returns the date exactly 12 months ago — updates daily.
entries.failure_modes: Result is a serial number — must be formatted as a date; adding months to January 31 returns the last day of February (not March 3); does not account for business days — use WORKDAY for business-day offsets.
entries.performance_notes: EDATE is a non-volatile date function. Faster than equivalent DATE(YEAR, MONTH+n, DAY) constructions and handles month-end edge cases correctly where manual month arithmetic fails.
entries.governance_notes: EDATE is the governed alternative to manual month arithmetic (=DATE(YEAR(A2), MONTH(A2)+3, DAY(A2))). Manual month arithmetic fails silently on month-end dates (Jan 31 + 3 months = Apr 31, which Excel resolves as May 1 incorrectly). Any rolling date window in a DDL workbook must use EDATE or EOMONTH, never manual arithmetic.

entries.name: Parameter Table
entries.what_it_does: Stores query variables (file paths, filter criteria, date ranges, thresholds) in a dedicated Excel table that Power Query reads before loading, making queries reusable and configurable without editing M code.
entries.intent: When a Power Query needs to be reconfigured for different runs (different dates, different folders, different filter values), store the variables in a Parameter Table so the query reads its own settings from a governed source.
entries.example:
PARAMETER TABLE (on Config tab):
ParameterName  | Value
FilePath       | C:\Data\Sales\2026\
StartDate      | 2026-01-01
MinAmount      | 500

IN POWER QUERY (M code):
let
  Params = Excel.CurrentWorkbook(){[Name="tbl_Parameters"]}[Content],
  FilePath = Params{[ParameterName="FilePath"]}[Value],
  Source = Folder.Files(FilePath)
in Source
entries.failure_modes: Parameter Table must be a named Excel Table (Ctrl+T) for reliable M code reference; parameter names must match exactly including case; if the table is on a different sheet than expected, the query may not find it; date values must be stored as text in ISO format to prevent serial number conversion.
entries.performance_notes: Parameter Table adds one table read at query load time — negligible overhead. Significantly reduces maintenance time compared to editing M code directly for each run.
entries.governance_notes: Any Power Query that runs against variable inputs (dates, paths, thresholds) must use a Parameter Table. Hard-coded values inside M code are the PQ equivalent of ANT-0003 (Hardcoded Values) — they make the query brittle and non-reusable. The Parameter Table must live on the Config tab alongside the Driver Table Architecture (ARC-0010).

entries.name: Append vs Merge
entries.what_it_does: Defines the structural difference between Append (stacking tables vertically — union) and Merge (joining tables horizontally — join), which are the two foundational data combination operations in Power Query.
entries.intent: When combining data from multiple sources in Power Query, use Append to stack rows from tables with the same structure, and Merge to join tables on a shared key column — never confuse the two.
entries.example:
APPEND (vertical stack — same columns):
January sales + February sales + March sales
→ One table with all rows, same columns
Use: Transform > Append Queries

MERGE (horizontal join — shared key):
Sales table [OrderID] joined to Products table [OrderID]
→ One table with columns from both, matched on OrderID
Use: Transform > Merge Queries
Join types: Left Outer, Inner, Full Outer, Left Anti, Right Anti
entries.failure_modes: Appending tables with different column names produces null values in mismatched columns; merging on a non-unique key produces a cartesian product (row explosion); Left Anti join is the governed pattern for finding orphan records — not a filter applied after a full join.
entries.performance_notes: Append is a lightweight union operation. Merge performance depends on join type and table size — Inner joins are fastest, Full Outer joins are most expensive. On large tables, filter before merging to reduce join cost.
entries.governance_notes: Every PQ data combination must explicitly declare whether it is an Append or a Merge and which join type applies. Undocumented merges are a governance finding — the join type determines which rows survive and must be intentional. Left Anti join is the governed pattern for exception reporting (records in A not in B).

entries.name: Rolling Window Pattern
entries.what_it_does: Constructs a dynamic date range that always covers a fixed trailing period (12 months, 13 weeks, 90 days) anchored to today, using EDATE and SEQUENCE to drive formulas, charts, and summaries without manual date updates.
entries.intent: When a dashboard or summary must always show the trailing N periods without manual date entry, use the Rolling Window Pattern to anchor all period references to TODAY() through EDATE and SEQUENCE.
entries.example:
-- Rolling 12-month header row
=EDATE(EOMONTH(TODAY(),0), -SEQUENCE(1,12,11,0))
Generates 12 month-end dates ending this month, left to right.

-- Rolling 12-month SUMIFS
=SUMIFS(Sales[Amount], Sales[MonthEnd], ">="&EDATE(TODAY(),-12), Sales[MonthEnd], "<="&TODAY())

-- Rolling window driven by a single Config cell
Config!B2 = 12 (months to show)
=EDATE(TODAY(), -Config!B2) -- window start, updates daily
entries.failure_modes: Rolling windows anchored to TODAY() recalculate on every workbook open — use a Config cell to freeze the anchor date for point-in-time reporting; EDATE on the last day of a month can produce unexpected results at month-end; rolling windows that cross fiscal year boundaries require additional logic for fiscal calendar alignment.
entries.performance_notes: TODAY() is volatile — it triggers recalculation on every workbook open. Anchor rolling windows to a Config cell date for workbooks where recalculation overhead is a concern.
entries.governance_notes: Any dashboard showing trailing periods must use the Rolling Window Pattern. Hardcoded date ranges in formulas or charts are a governance finding — they require manual updates and produce stale data silently. The Config cell anchor is mandatory for any workbook used for point-in-time reporting.

entries.name: Control Panel Pattern
entries.what_it_does: Consolidates all global workbook assumptions, toggles, thresholds, and user-selectable variables into a single labeled input grid on a dedicated tab, so every downstream formula reads from one governed source.
entries.intent: When a workbook has multiple user-configurable inputs that drive calculations across multiple tabs, build a Control Panel tab so all inputs are visible, labeled, and validated in one place.
entries.example:
CONTROL PANEL TAB (Config):
+-------------------+--------+------------------+
| Parameter         | Value  | Notes            |
+-------------------+--------+------------------+
| Report_Date       | 3/31/26| Override TODAY() |
| Tax_Rate          | 8.75%  | State + Local    |
| Tier1_Threshold   | 500    | Commission floor |
| LOD_Filter        | All    | All / L1 / L2    |
| Show_Inactive     | FALSE  | Toggle           |
+-------------------+--------+------------------+

Formulas reference: =Config[Value] via XLOOKUP or named ranges.
entries.failure_modes: Control Panel must be protected with only the Value column unlocked; parameter names must be stable — renaming breaks downstream formulas; dropdown validation on toggle cells prevents invalid entries; undocumented parameters accumulate over time — every row needs a Notes column entry.
entries.performance_notes: Zero computational overhead. The Control Panel is a lookup source, not a calculation engine. XLOOKUP against a 20-row parameter table is negligible.
entries.governance_notes: Every DDL workbook with more than 3 user-configurable inputs must have a Control Panel tab. Inputs scattered across formula cells are a governance finding. The Control Panel is the human-facing layer of the Driver Table Architecture (ARC-0010) — same principle, different audience. Users interact with the Control Panel; formulas interact with the Driver Table.

entries.name: Dynamic Spill Template
entries.what_it_does: Designs a workbook layout where all formula outputs use dynamic arrays that spill automatically, eliminating manual range adjustments when source data grows or shrinks.
entries.intent: When building a formula-driven output that must adapt to variable data lengths, design the layout around spill ranges so that adding rows to the source table automatically extends all downstream outputs without formula edits.
entries.example:
LAYOUT DESIGN:
- Source: tbl_Sales (Excel Table — auto-expands)
- Unique regions: =SORT(UNIQUE(tbl_Sales[Region]))  -- spills down
- Summary: =GROUPBY(tbl_Sales[Region], tbl_Sales[Amount], SUM)  -- spills down
- Header: =TRANSPOSE(SORT(UNIQUE(tbl_Sales[Quarter])))  -- spills right

KEY RULE: Leave blank columns/rows adjacent to every spill range.
Never place content in a cell that a spill range might expand into.
entries.failure_modes: #SPILL! error when another value blocks the spill range; spill ranges cannot overlap — plan layout to give each formula room to expand to its maximum likely size; spill ranges referenced by other formulas must use the # operator (e.g., A1#) not a fixed range.
entries.performance_notes: Dynamic spill layouts recalculate as a single array operation per formula — more efficient than equivalent dragged-down formula ranges. Source table growth triggers incremental recalculation only on affected ranges.
entries.governance_notes: Any workbook built for recurring use with variable data volumes must use the Dynamic Spill Template pattern. Fixed ranges that require manual extension when data grows are a governance finding. Every spill range must have documented maximum expected dimensions in the README so future maintainers can plan the layout.

entries.name: Ctrl+[ — Trace Precedents Jump
entries.what_it_does: Jumps the active cell selection directly to the precedent cells referenced in the current formula, bypassing manual scrolling and tab switching when auditing formula chains.
entries.intent: When auditing a formula and needing to inspect the source cells it references, use Ctrl+[ to jump directly to those cells rather than manually reading the formula and navigating.
entries.example:
1. Select a cell containing =XLOOKUP(A2, Config[Param], Config[Value])
2. Press Ctrl+[ 
3. Excel jumps to A2 (first precedent)
4. Press Ctrl+[ again to trace further back
5. Press Ctrl+] to jump to dependents (cells that USE the current cell)

For cross-sheet precedents:
1. Ctrl+[ jumps to the source sheet automatically
2. Ctrl+G (Go To) > Last to return to starting point
entries.failure_modes: Ctrl+[ only jumps to the first precedent — use Ctrl+Shift+{ to select ALL precedents at once; does not work on array formula results in older versions; cross-workbook precedents require the source workbook to be open.
entries.performance_notes: Keyboard shortcut — zero computational overhead. Significantly faster than reading formula text and manually navigating to source cells.
entries.governance_notes: Ctrl+[ and Ctrl+] (trace precedents / dependents) are the primary keyboard tools for workbook auditing. Any auditor reviewing a DDL workbook should be comfortable with both shortcuts. Pairs with Ctrl+Shift+{ (select all precedents) for full dependency mapping.

entries.name: One Version of the Truth
entries.what_it_does: Establishes the governance rule that any global variable — a rate, a threshold, a target date, a category list — must exist in exactly one cell across the entire workbook, with all other references pointing to that single source.
entries.intent: When a value is used in multiple places across a workbook, define it once in a named cell or Control Panel row and reference that location everywhere, so changing the value requires editing exactly one cell.
entries.example:
WRONG: Tax rate of 8.75% hardcoded in cells D4, G12, K8, and the dashboard formula.
Changing the rate requires finding and updating 4 places. One will be missed.

RIGHT:
Config tab, row 3: Tax_Rate | 8.75% | State + Local combined
Named range: Tax_Rate = Config!$B$3
All formulas: =Revenue * Tax_Rate
Changing the rate: edit Config!B3 once. Done.
entries.failure_modes: Named ranges can break if the referenced cell is deleted or moved — use structured table references instead of absolute cell addresses where possible; the 'one cell' rule applies to the definition, not to calculated derivations (e.g., monthly rate = annual rate / 12 is a derivation, not a violation).
entries.performance_notes: Zero computational overhead. Named range lookups are resolved at parse time, not calculation time.
entries.governance_notes: One Version of the Truth is the most fundamental governance convention in workbook design. Any value that appears in more than one hardcoded location is a governance finding. This convention is the human-readable complement to the Driver Table Architecture (ARC-0010) — the Driver Table stores business rules, One Version of the Truth stores the values those rules use.

entries.name: Dashboard Slicer Architecture
entries.what_it_does: Connects multiple PivotTables and PivotCharts to a unified set of Slicers so a single user selection filters all connected objects simultaneously, creating an interactive dashboard without VBA.
entries.intent: When building a multi-visual dashboard where all charts and tables must respond to the same filter selections, use connected Slicers to drive all PivotTables from a single interaction point.
entries.example:
SETUP:
1. Create all PivotTables from the same source table
2. Insert Slicer on one PivotTable (PivotTable Tools > Insert Slicer)
3. Right-click Slicer > Report Connections
4. Check all PivotTables the Slicer should control
5. Repeat for each filter dimension (Region, Quarter, Product)

RESULT: Clicking 'Q1' in the Quarter Slicer filters all charts and
tables simultaneously — no VBA, no helper columns, no formulas.
entries.failure_modes: All connected PivotTables must share the same data source — Slicers cannot connect PivotTables from different sources; Slicer formatting must be applied individually or via Slicer Style; Timeline Slicers require a date field formatted as a date (not text); Slicers do not work with non-PivotTable ranges.
entries.performance_notes: Slicer selection triggers a full PivotTable refresh on all connected objects simultaneously. On large source datasets (500K+ rows), Slicer response time degrades — consider Power Pivot Data Model for large-scale Slicer architectures.
entries.governance_notes: Any executive dashboard with more than one PivotChart must use Slicer Architecture for filter consistency. Dashboards where each chart is filtered independently are a UX and governance failure — users cannot trust that all visuals reflect the same selection. All Slicers must be labeled, positioned in a dedicated filter zone, and documented in the README.
